preFix.title: Fitting gimp braid to a chair
preFix.subtitle: Cover the staple line on an upholstered chair with a decorative braid trim
preFix.excerpt: How to fit gimp braid to the visible edge of an upholstered chair: measuring and cutting the braid, fixing with gimp pins or adhesive, mitring corners, and joining the ends neatly.

content.text: is the finishing step on a traditionally upholstered chair. The braid covers the line of staples or tacks at the fabric edge and gives the chair its polished appearance. The technique is the same whether the braid is fixed with
Finishing a newly upholstered show-wood chair frame where the fabric edge is visible.
Replacing worn or missing braid on an existing chair without re-covering the fabric.
Adding braid to a piano stool or footstool where the fabric meets the polished frame.

Measure the perimeter of the area to be trimmed, adding 50 mm at the start and end for the joins. Cut the braid with the upholstery shears rather than scissors; the shears cut the woven structure cleanly without fraying. Seal the cut ends immediately with a lighter flame passed briefly across the cut face to fuse the fibres.

Choose a starting point that will be at the back or underside of the chair. Fold the starting end of the braid under by 10 mm to give a clean turned end. Set one gimp pin through the braid at the fold with the tack hammer, tapping it home until the head is flush with the braid surface.

Work along the staple line, pressing the braid flat with one hand while setting gimp pins every 50 mm through the centre of the braid. On curved sections, reduce the pin spacing to 30 mm to keep the braid from lifting away from the fabric. Use the upholstery regulator to ease the braid into tight curves where needed.
Step 4, turn corners and join the end.

At corners, fold a mitred pleat in the braid and pin through the fold. At the finish, cut the braid 10 mm past the start point, fold the end under, and pin it down over the turned starting end. The join should be invisible from the front.
Step back and view the braid from a normal viewing distance before setting every pin. A braid that drifts above or below the staple line is visible immediately.
